stage-intentionally.label: Stage and Commit One Purpose
stage-intentionally.summary: Choose a coherent snapshot, review its staged content, and record it with a message that matches the boundary.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.title: Stage One File, Not Everything
try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

`index.html` and `styles.css` both changed, but the next snapshot is only about navigation. Stage `index.html` and list the staged names.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.hint: Choose only the paths that belong to this checkpoint.

help.concept: Path-specific staging selects one coherent change while leaving unrelated working-tree edits outside the next commit.
help.hint_1: Do not broaden the staging boundary just to make status quieter.
help.hint_2: Review the staged paths or patch before moving on.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..

printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }

printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>

try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.title: Inspect the Exact Staged Patch
try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

`index.html` is already staged while `styles.css` remains unstaged. Inspect the staged patch and then compare it with short status.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.hint: Inspect the exact version currently selected for the next commit.

help.concept: git diff --staged previews what the next commit would record, not every working-tree edit.
help.hint_1: A staged patch is different from the unstaged working-tree patch.
help.hint_2: Confirm that the selected content matches the task before recording it.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.title: Add a Second Related File
try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The staged navigation change also needs `nav.js`; `styles.css` is still unrelated. Stage `nav.js` and verify that exactly two files are staged.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.hint: Choose only the paths that belong to this checkpoint.

help.concept: A focused snapshot may contain several files when they serve the same change.
help.hint_1: Do not broaden the staging boundary just to make status quieter.
help.hint_2: Review the staged paths or patch before moving on.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- index.html

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- index.html

1.message: Did you review the staged path list and confirm that only the requested files are selected?

try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.title: Commit One Purpose and Leave the Rest
try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.prompt: You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository. Only `README.md` is staged; `styles.css` is an unrelated working-tree experiment. Commit the documentation change as `Clarify volunteer hours`, then use short status to confirm that the style edit remains outside the commit.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.hint: Record the staged documentation change without trying to make the whole repository quiet.

help.concept: A focused commit can finish one purpose while unrelated work remains in the working tree.
help.hint_1: The staged boundary is already prepared.
help.hint_2: After committing, short status should still name the unrelated style file.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Clarify volunteer hours"
git status --short

single-choice-1.title: Staging Area Purpose
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the staging area in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To prepare changes for a commit

multi-choice-1.title: A Coherent Snapshot
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which practices help keep a commit focused? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

options.a: Stage files that serve the same project result.
options.b: Review the staged snapshot before committing.
options.c: Stage every changed file so status becomes quiet.
options.d: Use a subject that describes the result.

multi-choice-2.title: Inspect the Staged Boundary
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which commands can help verify what is staged?
multi-choice-2.hint: Choose the one answer that describes this command or concept precisely.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Remove choices that act on a different repository layer or promise behavior Git does not provide.
help.hint_2: Prefer the exact definition used in this chapter over a nearby Git idea.

fill-blank-1.title: Write a Focused Commit Subject
fill-blank-1.prompt: The staged change adds event navigation. Complete the subject: `git commit -m "[blank1]"`.
fill-blank-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

stage-intentionally-sketch0.title: Choose One Purpose
stage-intentionally-sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
You already know how to stage a file. The new skill is deciding **which** files belong together. When navigation and palette edits are both valid, stage only the paths that serve the next project result.

A focused snapshot is easier to review, explain, and reverse than a bundle of unrelated work.

stage-intentionally-sketch1.title: Review the Proposed Snapshot
stage-intentionally-sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A staged diff answers one precise question: what would the next commit record?

```bash
git diff --staged -- index.html
```

This is the staged counterpart to the plain diff taught earlier. Review the content and the path list before committing.

stage-intentionally-sketch2.title: Keep Related Files Together
stage-intentionally-sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A focused commit is not necessarily one file. A navigation page and the script that makes it work may represent one result, while an unrelated style experiment should wait.

Use the staged path list to prove the boundary. The decision—not the number of files—is what makes the snapshot coherent.

stage-intentionally-sketch3.title: Commit the Boundary You Chose
stage-intentionally-sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
A clear subject completes the boundary. Write it as a concise action—`Clarify volunteer hours`, not `changes` or `worked on site`.

The commit records only the staged snapshot. Short status afterward should still show any unrelated edit you intentionally left outside. That remaining work is evidence that the focused boundary worked.
